description: Fundamental property of matter; can be positive or negative; quantized as multiples of e=1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C; conserved in all reactions
misconception: Students confuse charge with current; think charge flows like water

description: Rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor cross-section; I = Q/t
misconception: Students think current is the movement of protons; confuse with conventional direction

description: By convention current flows + to −, opposite to electron flow
misconception: Students think electrons flow from + to −; confuse conventional vs actual

description: Closed continuous path for current flow; must be unbroken for current to flow
misconception: Students think open circuit can still carry current

description: Measures current; ALWAYS in series; has very low resistance
misconception: Students connect ammeter in parallel (like voltmeter); confuse with voltmeter connection rule

description: Work done per unit positive charge moved between two points; drives current
misconception: Students confuse potential with current; think higher potential = more current always

description: Measures potential difference; ALWAYS in parallel; has very high resistance
misconception: Students connect voltmeter in series; confuse with ammeter connection rule

description: Source of electrical energy; converts chemical energy to maintain potential difference across terminals; has EMF and internal resistance
misconception: Students think battery provides current not energy; confuse EMF with terminal voltage

description: At constant temperature, V ∝ I for metallic conductors; the ratio V/I is constant = resistance
misconception: Students think Ohm's Law is universal (apply it to bulbs and diodes); miss the 'constant temperature' condition
change_flag: CORRECTED: circular prereq with E14 resolved

description: Opposition to flow of current; depends on material, length, area; R = V/I
misconception: Students think higher resistance = more heat always; confuse with resistivity
change_flag: CORRECTED: removed E13 from prereqs (was circular)

description: Slope of V-I graph = Resistance; straight line through origin = Ohmic conductor; curved = non-Ohmic
misconception: Students read slope as I/V not V/I; think curved V-I means no resistance; swap axes

description: Resistor: fixed resistance component; Rheostat: variable resistor to control current without changing voltage
misconception: Students think rheostat changes voltage; confuse with potentiometer

description: R ∝ length (L); R ∝ 1/area (A); R depends on material (ρ); R changes with temperature
misconception: Students think thicker wire has MORE resistance; forget temperature effect

description: Intrinsic material property; ρ = RA/L; independent of shape/size; increases with temperature for metals, decreases for semiconductors
misconception: Students confuse resistivity with resistance; think they are the same thing

description: Conductors: low ρ (metals, alloys); Insulators: very high ρ (glass, rubber); Alloys have higher ρ than pure metals — used in heating elements
misconception: Students think alloys conduct better than pure metals; confuse resistivity ranking

description: Standard symbols: cell, battery, resistor, variable resistor, ammeter, voltmeter, switch, bulb
misconception: Students draw bulb symbol for all resistors; mix up ammeter/voltmeter symbols

description: In series: same current through all; voltages add; R_eq = R1+R2+…; if one breaks, all fail
misconception: Students think voltage is same in series (parallel rule); forget R_eq is always LARGER than any individual R

description: In parallel: same voltage across all; currents add; 1/R_eq = 1/R1+1/R2+…; R_eq always less than smallest R
misconception: Students think R_eq in parallel is average of resistances; forget R_eq is SMALLER than any individual R

description: Series: simple wiring, one switch controls all, R increases; Parallel: independent operation, R decreases, used in homes
misconception: Students think series is used in homes because it's simpler; don't know why parallel is preferred

description: Current through resistance produces heat; H = I²Rt (Joule's Law); energy is dissipated as heat
misconception: Students think only high current causes heating; miss the role of resistance; confuse H=I²Rt with P=IV

description: Heat produced proportional to I², R, and time; doubling current quadruples heat; doubling R doubles heat
misconception: Students confuse H ∝ I with H ∝ I² (miss the square); think doubling current doubles heat

description: Electric iron, toaster, heater, bulb filament — all use Joule heating; material must have high melting point + high resistivity
misconception: Students think any metal can be used as filament; don't connect material properties to function

description: Tungsten filament: high melting point (~3500°C), high resistivity; inert gas fills bulb to prevent oxidation
misconception: Students think filament glows because of low resistance; confuse with heating elements

description: Safety device; thin wire with low melting point; melts when current exceeds rated value; breaks circuit
misconception: Students think fuse increases resistance to protect; confuse fuse rating with load power

description: Rate of electrical energy consumed; P=VI=I²R=V²/R; unit is Watt (1W=1J/s)
misconception: Students use only P=VI and forget P=I²R and P=V²/R forms; can't solve when only R and V given

description: 1 kWh = energy used by 1 kW appliance in 1 hour = 3.6×10⁶ J; used for billing
misconception: Students think kWh is a unit of power not energy; confuse kW and kWh

description: Overloading: too many appliances on same circuit exceed safe current; Short circuit: live and neutral touch, resistance drops to near zero, current surges
misconception: Students confuse overloading with short circuit; think fuse prevents short circuit (it only responds after)

description: Three wires: Live (red/brown), Neutral (black/blue), Earth (green); parallel wiring; 220V supply; separate circuits for lights and power
misconception: Students think domestic wiring is series; confuse live and neutral wire functions

description: Live: carries current at high potential; Neutral: at zero potential; Earth: safety — connected to ground to prevent shock
misconception: Students think neutral and earth are the same; don't understand why earth wire is needed

description: Region around magnet/current where magnetic force acts; vector quantity; represented by field lines
misconception: Students confuse magnetic field with gravitational field; think field only exists near poles

description: Closed curves showing direction of magnetic force; never intersect; density indicates field strength
misconception: Students think field lines are real physical entities; don't understand what 'density' means here

description: Form closed loops; direction from N to S outside magnet; never cross; crowded = stronger field
misconception: Students think field lines cross at poles; confuse direction inside vs outside magnet

description: Has two poles (N and S); cannot isolate a single pole; produces field lines from N to S externally
misconception: Students think cutting a magnet removes one pole; expect monopoles to exist

description: Like poles repel; unlike poles attract; same law as electric charges but no magnetic monopole
misconception: Students confuse magnetic attraction/repulsion rules with electric charge rules

description: Small pivoted magnet; aligns with Earth's magnetic field; N pole points geographic north
misconception: Students think compass N pole points to Earth's magnetic north pole (it actually points to geographic north = Earth's magnetic south pole)

description: Moving charges (current) create magnetic field around them — foundation of electromagnetism
misconception: Students think magnetic effect only occurs in special wires or electromagnets

description: Current in straight wire deflects nearby compass needle — proves electric current produces magnetic field; deflection reverses when current reverses
misconception: Students confuse cause and effect — think compass creates the field

concept_name: Magnetic Field due to Straight Wire
description: Field forms concentric circles around wire; direction by Right-Hand Thumb Rule; strength ∝ I, ∝ 1/distance
misconception: Students draw field lines as parallel lines; think field strength is constant at all distances

description: Thumb points in direction of current; curled fingers show direction of magnetic field circles around wire
misconception: Students point thumb along field direction instead of current direction; apply rule to wrong situations

concept_name: Magnetic Field due to Circular Loop
description: Field at centre is straight; field lines appear as if from a bar magnet; strength increases with turns
misconception: Students think circular loop field is same as straight wire field

description: Coil of insulated wire; field inside is uniform and parallel — like bar magnet; strength ∝ n (turns/length) and I
misconception: Students don't understand why solenoid field is uniform inside; think it's same as single loop

description: Soft iron core inside solenoid; magnetized only when current flows; strength increased by more turns, higher current, soft iron core
misconception: Students think permanent magnet is stronger than electromagnet; don't know what soft iron does

description: Conductor in magnetic field experiences force F = BIL sinθ; force perpendicular to both B and I
misconception: Students think force is along the current direction; forget it's perpendicular

description: Left hand: forefinger=B field, middle finger=current, thumb=force/motion; used for motors
misconception: Students confuse Left-Hand Rule (motor) with Right-Hand Rule (generator); apply wrong hand

concept_name: Reversal of Current and Field Direction
description: Reversing current in wire reverses direction of magnetic field produced around it
misconception: Students think reversing current only changes field strength, not direction

description: Alternative to Right-Hand Thumb Rule: rotate corkscrew in current direction — motion of screw = field direction
misconception: Students treat this as different from RHTR when it gives same result — creates confusion about which to use

description: Field with same magnitude and direction everywhere; represented by parallel equidistant field lines
misconception: Students think all magnetic fields are uniform; apply uniform-field formula to all situations

description: A device that converts electrical energy to mechanical (rotational) energy using force on current-carrying conductor in magnetic field
misconception: Students confuse motor with generator — mix up energy conversion direction

description: Rectangular coil in magnetic field; current from split-ring commutator; coil rotates due to torque; direction by FLHR
misconception: Students don't understand role of commutator; think coil can rotate without it

description: Changing magnetic flux through a coil induces EMF (Faraday's Law); changing B or moving conductor induces current
misconception: Students confuse with static magnetic field; think any field induces current (not changing field)

description: Converts mechanical energy to electrical energy using electromagnetic induction; rotating coil in magnetic field
misconception: Students think generator and motor are different devices (they are reverse of each other)

description: Sensitive instrument to detect small current; works on force on current-carrying coil in magnetic field
misconception: Students confuse galvanometer with ammeter; don't know galvanometer can detect zero current

description: Fuse and MCB protect circuits; fuse rating must match appliance; earthing prevents shock; overloading must be avoided
misconception: Students think higher-rated fuse = safer; confuse MCB with fuse function

concept_name: Series vs Parallel in Domestic Wiring
description: Domestic wiring uses parallel connections; each appliance gets full 220V; independent operation; equal voltage distribution
misconception: Students think domestic wiring is in series because devices are connected to same supply

description: Live and neutral wires touch → R→0 → huge current surge → fuse melts; caused by faulty insulation
misconception: Students think short circuit is same as open circuit; confuse which increases/decreases resistance
